What the Dying Really Need
nytimes_articles.abstract:
When the United States Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit ruled on Wednesday that terminally ill, mentally competent patients have a constitutional right to physician-assisted suicide, advocates of the right to die movement hailed it as a civil rights triumph. That is true, but the celebration must be tempered with respect, caution and careful planning. 

     Judge Stephen Reinhardt, in his majority opinion overruling a Washington State law forbidding physician-assisted suicide, launched a new era of civil liberties for the dying: "A competent, terminally ill adult, having lived nearly the full measure of his life, has a strong liberty interest in choosing a dignified and humane death." Thus in California and the eight other states under the court's jurisdiction, physicians have suddenly gained the right to prescribe lethal doses of medication for those who are suffering greatly.

A Freer Vietnam Brings Rise in Violent Crime
nytimes_articles.abstract:
When Tran Ngoc Bich, a 19-year-old honor student, was found bludgeoned to death in her own bedroom, apparently the victim of a burglar, it was a brutal example of the rise in violent crime in Vietnam. 

     Miss Bich, a sociology student at the Ho Chi Minh Institute of Marxist and Leninist Thought, was held up as a model for other students. Because she was so  young and her death so violent, the killing has shaken the authorities and citizens alike. But such brutality is not as unusual as it once was, say those who have watched violent crime climb as Vietnam moves from an authoritarian system to one that allows a freer way of life. And crimes like these have jolted Vietnamese into acknowledging the dark side of the country's rapid economic growth and emergence from years of international isolation.

Doing Major Time For a Minor Crime
nytimes_articles.abstract:
Every night around midnight, a dirty old van departs the Midtown South precinct and cruises lower Manhattan. This van will prowl through some of the nicest neighborhoods of the city and some of the worst. It will stay out all night, or until it is full. 

     The back of the van contains no seats, only a bare metal floor that smells faintly of vomit. As the evening progresses, this area will fill with members of every stratum of society, victims of a sweep by this old van and its convoy of unmarked escorts.

To Be 18 Years Old And Homeless
nytimes_articles.abstract:
I spent the first 21 years of my life in foster care in New York City. After  living with five foster families, I "graduated" from the system in 1991. Since then, I have also graduated from the State University of New York at Binghamton  and Columbia Law School, and have achieved the dream I nurtured since I was 10 years old. I am an advocate for children in foster care. 

     My sister, who spent 21 years mostly in group homes, began working in an entry-level position at a bank when she was 18. She stayed on after her discharge from foster care at 21 and is still there, supporting herself on a decent salary.

Irish Coffee and Chocolate Sorbet
nytimes_articles.abstract: This recipe came to The Times in 1996 as part of a St. Patrick’s Day menu from James O’Shea, a restaurateur in Connecticut. It’s 20 minutes of prep time for an Irish-tinged treat any day of the year. To serve it after dinner, you’ll want to make sure the sorbet is in the freezer in the morning. Serve it in a tulip glass with plenty of whipped cream, and Mr. O’Shea likes Jameson’s for the whisky. Sláinte!
